questions.stem: A linear programming problem is characterized by
options: a linear objective function and linear constraints
a linear objective with at least one quadratic constraint
a quadratic objective with linear constraints
questions.explanation: Linear programming means both the objective function and all the constraints are linear in the decision variables. If either the objective or a constraint were nonlinear, the problem would no longer be an LP. This linearity is what makes the feasible region a polyhedron and the optima occur at corners.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: To convert the constraint $2x_1 + 3x_2 \le 12$ to an equation in standard form, you introduce a slack variable $s \ge 0$ as
questions.explanation: A less-than-or-equal constraint is made into an equality by adding a nonnegative slack variable that takes up the unused amount: $2x_1 + 3x_2 + s = 12$ with $s \ge 0$. The slack measures how far the left side is below the bound. Subtracting a variable is used for $\ge$ constraints (a surplus variable), and squaring would make it nonlinear.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: For a $\ge$ constraint such as $x_1 + x_2 \ge 5$, the variable added to form an equality is called a
options: surplus variable, subtracted from the left side
artificial variable that may be negative
slack variable, added to the left side
questions.explanation: A greater-than-or-equal constraint becomes an equation by subtracting a nonnegative surplus variable: $x_1 + x_2 - s = 5$ with $s \ge 0$, where $s$ records the excess over the bound. A slack is added (for $\le$); a surplus is subtracted (for $\ge$). Both remain nonnegative.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: The feasible region of a linear program is always
questions.explanation: Each linear constraint defines a half-space, and the feasible region is the intersection of these half-spaces. An intersection of convex sets is convex, so the feasible region is a convex polyhedron. It may be empty, bounded, or unbounded, but it is never nonconvex.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming states that if an optimal solution exists, then
options: it occurs at a vertex (corner point) of the feasible region
the objective must be zero there
it occurs in the interior of the feasible region
questions.explanation: The fundamental theorem says an optimal value, if attained, is attained at a vertex (extreme point) of the feasible polyhedron. There may be ties along an edge, but at least one optimal point is a corner. This is why the simplex method searches only the finite set of vertices rather than the whole region.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Maximize $z = 3x + 2y$ subject to $x + y \le 4$, $x \le 3$, $x, y \ge 0$. The optimal value is
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Maximize $z = 4x + 3y$ subject to $2x + y \le 10$, $x + 3y \le 15$, $x, y \ge 0$. The optimal solution is
questions.explanation: Vertices: $(0,0)$, $(5,0)$ (from $2x+y=10$), $(0,5)$ (from $x+3y=15$), and the intersection of the two constraint lines. Solving $2x + y = 10$ and $x + 3y = 15$: from the first $y = 10 - 2x$, substitute to get $x + 30 - 6x = 15$, so $x = 3$, $y = 4$. Then $z(3,4) = 12 + 12 = 24$, beating $z(5,0) = 20$ and $z(0,5) = 15$.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: A constraint is said to be binding (active) at an optimal point when
options: it holds as a strict inequality there
it has been removed from the model
questions.explanation: A binding (active) constraint is one satisfied with equality at the optimum, so its slack variable is zero. A nonbinding constraint holds strictly, with positive slack, meaning there is room to spare. Binding constraints are the ones that actually limit the objective at the solution.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: A factory makes products A and B with unit profits 40 and 30. Each A uses 2 machine-hours, each B uses 1; only 100 machine-hours are available. Each unit (A or B) uses 1 unit of material, with 80 units available. Which is the correct LP?
questions.explanation: The objective is total profit, $40A + 30B$, to be maximized. Machine hours give $2A + B \le 100$ (A uses 2 each, B uses 1 each). Material gives $A + B \le 80$ (one unit each). Nonnegativity $A, B \ge 0$ completes it. Mixing the profit coefficients into the constraints or reversing the coefficients gives the wrong model.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: For the factory LP max $40A + 30B$ s.t. $2A + B \le 100$, $A + B \le 80$, $A,B \ge 0$, the maximum profit is
questions.explanation: Vertices: $(0,0)$, $(50,0)$ from $2A+B=100$, $(0,80)$ from $A+B=80$, and the intersection of $2A+B=100$ and $A+B=80$. Subtracting gives $A = 20$, then $B = 60$. Profits: $(50,0)\to 2000$, $(0,80)\to 2400$, $(20,60)\to 800 + 1800 = 2600$. The maximum is $2600$ at $(20,60)$.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Minimize $C = 2x + 3y$ subject to $x + y \ge 10$, $x + 2y \ge 14$, $x, y \ge 0$. The minimum cost is
questions.explanation: The feasible region is unbounded above, so the minimum sits at a vertex. Candidate vertices: $(14, 0)$ (from $x+2y=14$, and $x+y = 14 \ge 10$), $(0, 10)$ (from $x+y=10$, with $x+2y = 20 \ge 14$), and the intersection of $x+y=10$ and $x+2y=14$. Subtracting gives $y = 4$, $x = 6$. Costs: $(14,0)\to 28$, $(0,10)\to 30$, $(6,4)\to 12 + 12 = 24$. Minimum is $24$ at $(6,4)$.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: The simplex method finds an optimal solution by
options: differentiating the objective and setting it to zero
checking every point of the feasible region
moving from one vertex of the feasible region to an adjacent one that improves the objective
questions.explanation: The simplex method walks along edges of the feasible polyhedron, moving from a vertex to an adjacent vertex that improves (or holds) the objective, until no improving move remains. Because the optimum lies at a vertex, this finite search suffices. It does not test all points, and calculus-style differentiation does not apply to this piecewise-linear setting.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: If the feasible region of a maximization LP is unbounded in the direction in which the objective increases, then the LP
options: has no finite optimal value (it is unbounded)
must have its optimum at the origin
questions.explanation: When the feasible set extends without bound in a direction that keeps increasing the objective, the objective can be made arbitrarily large, so there is no finite maximum. The LP is then called unbounded. This is different from infeasibility, where the feasible region is empty.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Consider the Wyndor-type LP: max $z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$ s.t. $x_1 \le 4$, $2x_2 \le 12$, $3x_1 + 2x_2 \le 18$, $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$. The optimal value is
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: How many slack variables are needed to put the following into standard form: max $z = x + y$ s.t. $x + 2y \le 6$, $3x + y \le 9$, $x, y \ge 0$?
questions.explanation: Each $\le$ inequality constraint (other than the sign restrictions) gets one slack variable. There are two such constraints, $x + 2y \le 6$ and $3x + y \le 9$, so two slack variables are introduced: $x + 2y + s_1 = 6$ and $3x + y + s_2 = 9$. The nonnegativity conditions do not require slacks.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: A vertex (extreme point) of a polyhedral feasible region in two variables is
options: a point where two constraint boundaries intersect and that lies in the feasible region
questions.explanation: In two dimensions a vertex of the feasible region is a corner where two of the boundary lines cross, provided that crossing point satisfies all the constraints. Interior points and points partway along an edge are not vertices, and the centroid is generally not a corner.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: When an LP has alternative optimal solutions (a tie along an edge), it means
options: the objective contour is parallel to a binding constraint edge
questions.explanation: Multiple optima occur when the level line of the objective is parallel to one of the binding constraint edges. Then every point along that edge gives the same optimal value, so there is a whole segment of optimal solutions (with the corner endpoints among them). This requires neither infeasibility nor nonlinearity.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: In the standard form of an LP for the simplex method, the variables are required to be
options: nonnegative, with all functional constraints written as equalities
questions.explanation: Standard form requires all decision (and slack or surplus) variables to be nonnegative and all functional constraints to be equalities, typically with a nonnegative right-hand side. Variables are not required to be integers or bounded by $1$; a free variable would first be rewritten as a difference of two nonnegative variables.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: At the optimal vertex $(3,4)$ of max $z = 4x + 3y$ s.t. $2x + y \le 10$, $x + 3y \le 15$, $x,y \ge 0$, which constraints are binding?
questions.explanation: At $(3,4)$: $2x + y = 6 + 4 = 10$, so the first constraint is met with equality (binding). And $x + 3y = 3 + 12 = 15$, so the second is also binding. Both functional constraints have zero slack here, which is exactly why this corner is where the two boundary lines cross. The nonnegativity constraints are slack since $x, y > 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: An LP whose constraints have no point in common (the feasible region is empty) is called
questions.explanation: If no point satisfies all the constraints simultaneously, the feasible region is empty and the LP is infeasible: there is nothing to optimize. This differs from an unbounded LP, which has feasible points but no finite optimum. Degeneracy refers to a vertex where more constraints than necessary are binding.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Maximize $z = x + y$ over the same factory feasible set: $2x + y \le 100$, $x + y \le 80$, $x, y \ge 0$. The maximum of $z = x + y$ is
questions.explanation: Here the objective $x + y$ is itself one of the constraints' left sides. Since $x + y \le 80$ for all feasible points, the maximum possible value of $x + y$ is $80$, achieved anywhere on that binding edge, for instance at $(0,80)$ or $(20,60)$. The other constraint $2x + y \le 100$ does not raise this bound.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Which of the following is a redundant constraint in the system $x \le 5$, $x \le 8$, $x \ge 0$ (with $y \ge 0$ free otherwise)?
questions.explanation: A redundant constraint can be dropped without changing the feasible region. Since $x \le 5$ already implies $x \le 8$, the constraint $x \le 8$ adds nothing and is redundant. Removing $x \le 5$ or $x \ge 0$ would enlarge the feasible set, so those are not redundant.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: In a graphical two-variable LP, the optimal corner is found by
options: taking the average of all vertices
sliding the objective level line as far as possible in the improving direction until it last touches the feasible region
choosing the vertex nearest the origin
questions.explanation: Graphically, the objective $z = cx + dy$ has parallel level lines. To maximize, you push this line in the direction of increasing $z$ as far as it can go while still touching the feasible region; the last contact is at an optimal vertex (or edge). The nearest vertex to the origin or an average of vertices has no special optimality meaning.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: For the LP max $z = 5x + 4y$ s.t. $6x + 4y \le 24$, $x + 2y \le 6$, $x, y \ge 0$, the optimal solution is at
questions.explanation: Vertices: $(0,0)\to 0$, $(4,0)$ from $6x+4y=24 \to 20$, $(0,3)$ from $x+2y=6 \to 12$, and the intersection of $6x+4y=24$ and $x+2y=6$. From the second, $x = 6 - 2y$; substituting: $6(6-2y) + 4y = 24$, so $36 - 8y = 24$, $y = 1.5$, $x = 3$. Then $z(3,1.5) = 15 + 6 = 21$, the maximum.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: The role of the nonnegativity constraints $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$ in a typical product-mix LP is to
options: force the objective to be positive
restrict the solution to the first quadrant, since negative production is meaningless
questions.explanation: Nonnegativity confines the decision variables to the first quadrant, reflecting that you cannot produce a negative quantity of a product. They bound the feasible region from below in each coordinate but do not by themselves force the objective sign, guarantee uniqueness, or create unboundedness.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Solve graphically: maximize $z = 5x + 4y$ subject to $x + y \le 5$, $x \le 4$, $x, y \ge 0$.
questions.solution: The relevant vertices are $(0,0)$, $(4,0)$, $(4,1)$, and $(0,5)$. Evaluating the objective, the maximum occurs at the point $(4,${{1}}$)$, where $z =$ {{2}}. At this optimum the constraint $x + y \le 5$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Vertices and values: $(0,0)\to 0$, $(4,0)\to 20$, $(4,1)$ where $x=4$ meets $x+y=5 \to 20 + 4 = 24$, $(0,5)\to 20$. The maximum is $z = 24$ at $(4,1)$. There $x + y = 5$, so that constraint is binding (zero slack).
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Put the constraints $3x + 2y \le 12$ and $x + 4y \le 8$ into standard form for the simplex method.
questions.solution: Adding one slack variable to each $\le$ constraint gives $3x + 2y +$ {{1}} $= 12$ and $x + 4y + s_2 = 8$. Both slack variables must be {{2}}. In total this requires {{3}} slack variables.
questions.explanation: Each $\le$ constraint gets a nonnegative slack added: $3x + 2y + s_1 = 12$ and $x + 4y + s_2 = 8$, with $s_1, s_2 \ge 0$. Two constraints means two slack variables.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Find the optimum of max $z = 2x + 5y$ subject to $x + y \le 6$, $x + 3y \le 12$, $x, y \ge 0$.
questions.solution: Solving the two constraint lines together gives the vertex $(${{1}}$, 3)$. Comparing objective values at the vertices, the maximum value of $z$ is {{2}}, and at that point the number of binding functional constraints is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Intersecting $x + y = 6$ and $x + 3y = 12$: subtracting gives $2y = 6$, so $y = 3$, $x = 3$. Vertices and values: $(6,0)\to 12$, $(0,4)\to 20$, $(3,3)\to 6 + 15 = 21$. Maximum is $21$ at $(3,3)$, where both functional constraints are binding (2 of them).
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: Minimize $C = 3x + 2y$ subject to $2x + y \ge 8$, $x + y \ge 6$, $x, y \ge 0$.
questions.solution: The binding pair $2x + y = 8$ and $x + y = 6$ meet at $(2,${{1}}$)$. Checking the vertices of the unbounded region, the minimum cost is {{2}}. Because the feasible region is unbounded yet the cost is bounded below, the minimum is attained at a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Solving $2x + y = 8$ and $x + y = 6$: subtracting gives $x = 2$, then $y = 4$. Candidate vertices: $(0,8)\to 16$, $(6,0)\to 18$, $(2,4)\to 6 + 8 = 14$. The minimum is $14$ at $(2,4)$, a vertex of the region.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: State the key facts about linear-programming feasible regions and optima.
questions.solution: Because every constraint is linear, the feasible region is a {{1}} set. By the fundamental theorem, if an optimum exists it is attained at a {{2}} of the region. The method that moves between adjacent corners to improve the objective is the {{3}} method.
questions.explanation: Linear constraints make the feasible region a convex polyhedron. The fundamental theorem of LP places any attained optimum at a vertex (corner/extreme point), and the simplex method exploits this by walking between adjacent vertices in an improving direction.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization

questions.stem: A diet problem requires choosing amounts $x, y \ge 0$ of two foods to minimize cost $C = 4x + 3y$ subject to nutrient constraints $x + 2y \ge 8$ and $3x + 2y \ge 12$.
questions.solution: The two nutrient lines intersect at $(2,${{1}}$)$. Evaluating cost at the vertices, the minimum cost is {{2}}. This kind of model, with $\ge$ constraints and a cost to minimize, has surplus variables that are {{3}} from the left sides to reach standard form.
questions.explanation: Solving $x + 2y = 8$ and $3x + 2y = 12$: subtracting gives $2x = 4$, so $x = 2$, $y = 3$. Vertices: $(8,0)\to 32$, $(0,6)\to 18$, $(2,3)\to 8 + 9 = 17$. Minimum cost is $17$ at $(2,3)$. For $\ge$ constraints, surplus variables are subtracted to form equalities.
questions.source: in the style of Chong and Zak, An Introduction to Optimization
